perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time without cause upon written notice to the other party: provided that each party's obligations with respect to Confidential Information disclosed during the term of this Agreement will survive any such termination.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time without cause upon verbal notice to the other party: provided that each party's obligations with respect to Confidential Information disclosed during the term of this Agreement will not survive any such termination.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'written notice' to 'verbal notice' and stating that obligations will not survive termination creates a contradiction because it eliminates the necessary written record and the continued protection of confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Statute of Frauds (Varies by state, using Delaware as in the contract)
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware's Statute of Frauds requires certain agreements to be in writing to be enforceable. Modifying the contract to allow verbal termination notice and negate the survival clause undermines the protections afforded to confidential information, making the terms vague and possibly unenforceable under Delaware law.

(a) All bonds, specialties, and notes in writing, payable to any person, or order, or assigns, may be assigned, or indorsed, and the assignee, or indorsee, his or her executors, administrators, or assigns, may again assign, or indorse the same, as often as desired. The assignees, or indorsees, or their executors, or administrators, may, in their own name, sue for and recover the money due on the bonds, specialties, or notes. All assignments of bonds or specialties shall be under hand and seal, and executed before at least one credible witness. (b) Assignors, or indorsers, or their executors, or administrators may not release or discharge any sum due by the bonds, specialties, or notes, after the date of the assignment, and no release, receipt, or discharge from him, her or them made after the date of the assignment shall be good or available. perturbation.justification: The modification from 'written notice' to 'verbal notice' and negating the survival clause creates a significant contradiction. Delaware Code Title 6, Chapter 27 addresses contracts, including assignment of bonds, specialties, and notes which 'shall be under hand and seal, and executed before at least one credible witness.' While this snippet does not explicitly address termination notices or confidentiality, the emphasis on written documentation and formal execution suggests a legal preference for written agreements, which the changed text undermines by allowing verbal termination and negating confidentiality survival. The original text ensures a written record of termination and continued protection of confidential information. The altered text eliminates both, creating a strong potential for unenforceability under legal standards favoring written documentation.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party will maintain the confidentiality of the Confidential Information of the disclosing party with at least the same degree of care that it uses to protect its own confidential and proprietary information, but no less than a reasonable degree of care under the circumstances.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party will maintain the confidentiality of the Confidential Information of the disclosing party with such care as it deems adequate, based on its current resources.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing the 'reasonable degree of care' standard with 'such care as it deems adequate' lowers the standard of care and removes objectivity.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) - Varies by state, widely adopted.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain trade secret confidentiality. Allowing the receiving party to determine 'adequate care' based on resources permits a potentially insufficient standard that may not meet the 'reasonable' threshold required by law, potentially impacting trade secret protection.

UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the UTSA, as interpreted by the LLM, by replacing the 'reasonable degree of care' standard with a subjective 'such care as it deems adequate' standard. The Law Explanation states that this change permits a potentially insufficient standard that may not meet the 'reasonable' threshold required by law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, from California Code CIV 3426.1, defines 'improper means' which can include a 'breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. By lowering the standard of care, the modified contract increases the risk of such a breach, potentially contradicting the intent of the UTSA to protect trade secrets through reasonable measures, thus a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Unless a shorter period is indicated in the applicable CITR, the disclosing party will not insert any claims of breach of this Agreement or misappropriation of trade secrets against the receiving party arising from the receiving party's disclosure of the disclosing party's Confidential Information made more than five <PAGE> 2 (5) years from the date of the CITR under which such information was disclosed.
perturbation.changed_text: The disclosing party will not insert any claims of breach of this Agreement or misappropriation of trade secrets against the receiving party arising from the receiving party's disclosure of the disclosing party's Confidential Information, regardless of the date of disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 5-year limit and stating that there can *never* be a claim of breach introduces a clause that would be against public policy. It allows for the possibility of perpetual misappropriation with no legal recourse.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Statute of Limitations for Trade Secret Misappropriation (Varies by state, using Delaware as in the contract)
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware's statute of limitations for trade secret misappropriation provides a specific timeframe (3 years after the misappropriation is discovered or should have been discovered) within which a claim must be brought. The modified text, by negating the ability to bring a claim 'regardless of the date of disclosure', directly contradicts this statutory limitation, and would likely be found unenforceable as against public policy and the law.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the original contract's 5-year limit on claims of breach or misappropriation, stating that such claims can *never* be brought, 'regardless of the date of disclosure.' The Law Explanation states that Delaware has a statute of limitations for trade secret misappropriation, which implies that claims must be brought within a specific timeframe. Therefore, removing the possibility of a claim based on the disclosure date directly contradicts this principle of a time limit for claims. The lack of scraped law snippets doesn't negate the core contradiction because the Law Explanation specifies the existence of a statute of limitations, which the changed text violates by removing any time constraints on the action. This modification introduces a clause against public policy, allowing for perpetual misappropriation without legal recourse, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be governed by laws of the State of Delaware without reference to conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be governed by the laws of a country chosen at random by a coin flip at the time of any dispute, without reference to conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the jurisdiction from Delaware to a randomly selected country at the time of dispute creates massive uncertainty regarding which laws apply and the enforceability of the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Enforceability and Certainty of Terms
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a statute, the Restatement (Second) of Contracts reflects common law principles that require a contract to have reasonably certain terms to be enforceable. The selection of governing law at the time of dispute makes it impossible to determine the applicable laws *ex ante*, creating uncertainty and making the contract potentially unenforceable because key terms are indefinite. While a choice of law clause is generally respected, this clause is self-defeating by design.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: contract | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you contract A contract is an agreement between parties , creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law . The basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent , expressed by a valid offer and acceptance ; adequate consideration ; capacity ; and legality . In some states , elements of consideration can be satisfied by a valid substitute. Possible remedies for breach of contract include general damages , consequential damages , reliance damages , and specific performance . Background : Contracts are promises that the law will enforce. Contract law is generally governed by state common law , and while general overall contract law is common throughout the country, some specific court interpretations of a particular element of the contract may vary between the states. If a promise is breached, the law provides remedies to the harmed party, often in the form of monetary damages, or in limited circumstances, in the form of specific performance of the promise made. Elements -- Consideration and Mutual Assent Contracts arise when a duty comes into existence, because of a promise made by one of the parties. To be legally binding as a contract, a promise must be exchanged for adequate consideration. There are two different theories or definitions of consideration: Bargain Theory of Consideration and Benefit-Detriment theory of consideration. Benefit-Detriment Under the b enefit-detriment theory, an adequate consideration exists only when a promise made to the benefit of the promisor or to the detriment of the promisee, which reasonably and fairly induces the promisor to make a promise for something else for the promisee. For example, promises that are purely gifts are not considered enforceable because the personal satisfaction the grantor of the promise may receive from the act of generosity is normally not considered sufficient detriment to constitute adequate consideration. Bargain-for-Exchange Under Bargain-for-Exchange theory of consideration, adequate consideration exists when a promisor makes a promise in return for something else. Here, the essential condition is that the promisor was given something specifically to induce the promise being made. In other words, the bargain for exchange theory is different from the detriment-benefit theory in that the focus in bargain for exchange theory seems to be the parties’ motive for making the promises and the parties’ subjective mutual assent, while in detriment benefit theory, the focus seems to be an objective legal detriment or benefit to the parties. Governing Laws Contracts are mainly governed by state statutory and common (judge-made) law and private law (i.e. the private agreement). Private law principally includes the terms of the agreement between the parties who are exchanging promises. This private law may override many of the rules otherwise established by state law. Statutory law, such as the Statute of Fraud , may require some kinds of contracts be put in writing and executed with particular formalities, for the contract to be enforceable. Otherwise, the parties may enter into a binding agreement without signing a formal written document. For example, Virginia Supreme Court has held in Lucy v. Zehmer that even an agreement made on a piece of napkin can be considered a valid contract, if the parties were both sane, and showed mutual assent and consideration. Most of the principles of the common law of contracts are outlined in the Restatement of Law, Second Contracts published by the American Law Institute. The Uniform Commercial Code , whose original articles have been adopted in nearly every state, represents a body of statutory law that governs important categories of contracts. The main articles that deal with the law of contracts are Article 1 (General Provisions) and Article 2 (Sales) . Sections of Article 9 (
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a significant legal contradiction. The original text specifies Delaware law, providing certainty. The changed text introduces a random selection of a country's law at the time of a dispute. The Law Explanation states that contracts require reasonably certain terms to be enforceable. The changed text makes it impossible to determine applicable laws beforehand, contradicting this principle. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that contracts are agreements creating mutual obligations enforceable by law, and that the basic elements include mutual assent. The uncertainty introduced by the changed text undermines the element of mutual assent regarding the governing law, as parties cannot reasonably agree to something that is determined randomly in the future. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition because the changed text deviates from legally recognized requirements for enforceability and certainty in contract terms.
